name: Íscar, Olmedo, and Mojados Loop
name_original: Olmedo desde Íscar – circular por Mojados
description: Cycle the moderate 31.9-mile Olmedo from Íscar loop, exploring historic towns like Olmedo and Mojados with 676 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Íscar are set within Spain's Tierra de Pinares region, characterized by extensive pine forests and varied terrain. This landscape offers scenic routes with natural shade and a tranquil atmosphere, ideal for long-distance rides. The area features a mix of flat sections for speed and undulating countryside, providing diverse cycling experiences. Quiet country and secondary roads are prevalent, allowing cyclists to explore with minimal traffic interruption. Best road cycling routes around Íscar The most popular road cycling route is Wet from Olmedo – circular by Matapozuelos, an easy 28.5 miles (45.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 47 minutes to complete. This route offers a pleasant ride through the region's characteristic pine forests. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Olmedo from Íscar – circular via Mojados, a moderate 31.9 miles (51.3 km) path. This route connects towns through varied countryside, showcasing the region's diverse terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Peñafiel from Íscar – circular via Cogeces del Monte and Cuéllar, a 66.1 miles (106.4 km) trail leading through undulating countryside, often completed in about 4 hours 25 minutes. Road cycling around Íscar is defined by pine forests, quiet secondary roads, and varied terrain. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more moderate challenges. The routes in Íscar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 60 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Íscar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Íscar?
A: The Íscar region, nestled in Spain's Tierra de Pinares, offers a diverse road cycling experience. You'll find extensive pine forests providing natural shade and a tranquil atmosphere, ideal for long-distance rides. The terrain features a mix of flat sections perfect for speed, and undulating countryside that will test your legs, ensuring a varied and engaging ride.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Íscar area?
A: There are over 10 distinct road cycling routes around Íscar, catering to various preferences and skill levels. These routes explore the region's characteristic pine forests and connect charming towns through varied countryside.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Íscar?
A: Yes, Íscar offers several easy road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170266290/wet-from-olmedo-circular-by-matapozuelos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Wet from Olmedo – circular by Matapozuelos&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 28.5-mile (45.9 km) trail that provides a pleasant ride through the region's characteristic pine forests, making it a great option for those seeking a less strenuous experience.

Q: What are some moderate road cycling challenges around Íscar?
A: For those looking for a moderate challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170286431/olmedo-from-iscar-circular-via-mojados" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo from Íscar – circular via Mojados&lt;/a&gt; offer a good balance. This 31.9-mile (51.3 km) path connects towns through varied countryside, showcasing the region's diverse terrain and providing a rewarding ride.

Q: Are there any longer or more challenging road cycling routes from Íscar?
A: Yes, for longer rides, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170311889/penafiel-from-iscar-circular-via-cogeces-del-monte-and-cuellar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñafiel from Íscar – circular via Cogeces del Monte and Cuéllar&lt;/a&gt;. This route spans 66.1 miles (106.4 km) and leads through undulating countryside, providing a significant endurance test for experienced cyclists.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see along the road cycling routes near Íscar?
A: Road cycling routes around Íscar can lead you to several historical and natural points of interest. You might encounter the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813983" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coca Castle&lt;/a&gt;, a 15th-century example of Spanish Gothic-Mudejar architecture, or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3505363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Center of Olmedo&lt;/a&gt;. The picturesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5597863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megeces Bridge&lt;/a&gt; over the Cega River is another notable landmark.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Íscar?
A: The road cycling routes in Íscar are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the tranquil atmosphere of the pine forests, the varied terrain that keeps rides interesting, and the prevalence of quiet secondary roads that allow for enjoyable cycling with minimal traffic.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Íscar?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Íscar are designed as circular paths, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170266290/wet-from-olmedo-circular-by-matapozuelos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Wet from Olmedo – circular by Matapozuelos&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170286431/olmedo-from-iscar-circular-via-mojados" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo from Íscar – circular via Mojados&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular choices for exploring the region.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Íscar?
A: The Íscar region, with its natural shade from pine forests, can be enjoyable for road cycling across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for long rides. While specific weather conditions vary, the tranquil atmosphere and varied terrain make it a compelling destination for much of the year.

Q: Can I find routes that combine historical sites with natural scenery?
A: Absolutely. The region's routes often weave through both natural landscapes and areas rich in history. For example, you can plan a ride that passes by the medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813983" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coca Castle&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3505363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Olmedo&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to combine your passion for cycling with cultural exploration.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by the Megeces Bridge?
A: Yes, the 16th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5597863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megeces Bridge&lt;/a&gt; over the Cega River is a picturesque landmark that can be incorporated into your road cycling routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38149650" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuéllar Castle – Megeces Bridge loop from Chañe&lt;/a&gt; is one such route that features this historical structure.
tips: The strategic town of Íscar in the region of Tierra de Pinares. The city has a defensive castle on top of a hill that dominates the city and the landscape around it with a history of Moorish and Christian heritage.

The bridge over the Cega River, built between 1515 and 1516, is a remarkable structure commissioned by the Archpriest of Cogeces, Juan Díaz de Mirena. It consists of a single limestone semicircular arch and is twelve meters long. In the lower part, it has buttresses, and its railing is decorated with large semicircular stones. Inside the arch, fragments of the old wooden framework used during its construction are preserved.

Valladolid town where we find two beautiful churches built by mixing brick and masonry.
The Church of San Juan, Mudejar from the 13th and 14th centuries, in which its beautiful tower stands out, and the doorway with a pointed horseshoe arch.
The other church, very close to the path of the Cega river, is that of Santa María, Mudejar and from the 14th century.

Once a walled city, Olmedo retains some of its medieval charm, dating back to the Castilian and Leonese Mudejar period with several churches and heritage buildings located near the Plaza Mayor. It is a good place to stop for a coffee and admire the history.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Olmedo from Íscar – circular via Mojados route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved surfaces, making it ideal for road bikes. You'll be cycling through an area characterized by historical towns and the agricultural landscapes typical of the Valladolid province.

faqs.question: What can I see along this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route connects several towns rich in history and cultural heritage. You'll pass by notable landmarks such as Íscar Castle, the Megeces Bridge, and the San Juan Church in Mojados. In Olmedo, you can explore the City Walls and Historic Center, known for its Mudejar churches and literary significance.

faqs.question: Is the Olmedo from Íscar – circular via Mojados route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While it's not overly challenging, it covers approximately 51 kilometers with around 206 meters of elevation gain. It's best suited for cyclists who are comfortable with this distance and some changes in elevation, rather than absolute beginners.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from Íscar. While specific parking details for the exact trailhead are not provided, Íscar is a town where you should be able to find public parking options to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route would generally be during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be quite hot in the Valladolid province, and winter might bring colder, potentially wet conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route primarily on public roads, dogs are generally allowed if kept under control and on a leash, especially in urban areas. However, always be mindful of local regulations and other road users.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths between towns.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns like Íscar, Mojados, and Olmedo, you will find opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and other services to refuel and rest.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Madrid Way of Saint James, Senda del Cega, and Senda del Ermitaño, among others.

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for road cycling in the Olmedo area?
faqs.answer: Given it's a road cycling route, bring your road bike, appropriate cycling attire, a helmet, water bottles, and basic repair tools. Depending on the season, consider layers for varying temperatures and sun protection.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Due to its moderate difficulty, distance of over 50 km, and use of public roads, this route is generally more suitable for families with older, experienced teenage cyclists rather than young children or beginners.
